How do they ensure that similar data
is not appear in the training data?
So people have tried a bunch of different things.
This actually might be a good thing to talk about
at some point when you talk about data curation
or things like this.
The first thing is you actually create the data.
So similar is actually okay, right?
Because if it appears everywhere on the internet,
GPD4 will learn it.
The problem is like the exact same thing appears.
So number one, how do we prevent this from happening?
Number two, how do we even tell that it did happen?
So some things that people do to tell that it did happen
is it makes small perturbations to the test data
and test whether that drops the model score by a whole lot.
There's actually a paper, I don't know if I can find it
immediately, but there was a paper recently
that just swapped the order of outputs in MMLU
and saw that the accuracy went down
for some language models.
So that should make no difference whatsoever
because you're just changing the order of answers,
but it does outputs to go down.
If that's a case, it's a pretty clear sign that it's leaking.
Other things that people do are change the input a little bit.
So like change the number and the math problem
to be a slightly different value
and see if that hurts the accuracy overall.
And like making these little perturbations
that shouldn't change the accuracy
and then if they do significantly,
then you think there's a problem.
So I think that's a basic tool to diagnose this.
How do you prevent it from happening?
There's really simple and silly things
that you can do like zip the file
and like put a password on the file.
And then like a scraper, even if it's scraping
all of GitHub for training data,
it won't scrape your zip
and password protected file, right?
So that's kind of a first line of defense.
It doesn't work if someone puts it like,
someone puts it somewhere in unzipped format.
So that's a problem.
But there are things that you can do like that.
Another thing you can do is just not reveal
your data whatsoever.
So you can keep a private version of the data.
You can keep a private version of the data
and not let anybody else see the outputs.
So yeah, it's pretty tricky.
Yeah.
Yeah.
How do you control for test complexity?

the number of hops that are required

which keywords have been contained by more than

I don't have a whole lot about instruction tuning
because it's, you know, conceptually pretty simple,
but I would like to talk about all of it.
So basic instruction tuning was proposed almost simultaneously
by people at Google and people at Hugging Face.
The way it works is you have tasks
and you train on lots of tasks where you append the prompt
and you append the prompt, you append the input
and then you just try to train to generate that approach.
And so this is this contrast from like base language model
training because you're still training a language model
based on a prompt and an output,
but you're specifically formatting them
in a particular way.
So it corresponds to solving tasks.
It's essentially supervised training
but supervised training over many, many tasks,
fine tuning over many, many tasks.
The interesting thing that these papers showed was
that basically if you do this instruction tuning,
you do well not only on the task set you trained on
but also on new tasks that you didn't train on.
And so this is now really like important.
It's incorporated in every serious language model
that's used in a kind of like production setting nowadays.
And all, yeah, so I think that's the basic idea here.
The basic idea here.
You can also do things like learn to in context learn.
So we talked about in context learning.
So in context learning instead of giving just a prompt,
you give training examples in the context.
And so that's what you do in this paper here as well.
You sample a whole bunch of training examples,
you append them to the context and then you train the model.
And so why is this good?
This is good because it will train the model
that's better in context learning basically.
So if you want to provide these training examples
and you can train it like that.
So these are the two basic ways
of doing instruction tuning.
I'll all came out around the same time.
